Hearing Re: DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND PORTIER, LLC's MOTION TO TRANSFER.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. The Court states Plaintiff is to pay the transfer fees.

Uber Technologies, Inc. and Portier LLC's (Uber Eats defendants) CCP 397(c) motion to transfer venue to Santa Barbara County Superior Court is granted.

An Uber Eats driver killed plaintiffs' decedent. The collision was in Santa Barbara County, where decedent lived. Defendant driver, plaintiffs and at least 19 non-party witnesses reside in Santa Barbara County. (Memo. 5:17-14:2.) Counsel for both sides are in Southern California. Uber Eats' San Francisco headquarters is the case's only nexus here. Thus, "the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice" will "be promoted by" transfer of venue to Santa Barbara County. (CCP 397(c).) Any resulting inconvenience to Uber witnesses is waived by this motion.

Plaintiffs argue that defendant driver's criminal convictions "extinguish any factual issues in relation to the motor collision," but this speculation is supported by no legal authority and little analysis. (See Opp. 6:1-7:17.) In any event, the collision facts will only be part of the evidence.

Order signed.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CHECKR, INC's MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Checkr, Inc.'s motion to strike the first amended complaint's (FAC) punitive damages allegations is denied. As ruled on Checkr's demurrer, the FAC pleads viable claims. The FAC details a scheme to perform inadequate background checks leading to dangerous Uber Eats drivers - a scheme perpetrated in part by Checkr's management - that could result in punitive damages. (See, e.g., id. at 11:46-23:16; Civil Code 3294.) The FAC's allegations could eventually prove meritless, but that is not for resolution at the pleading stage.

Order signed.

MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DOUGLAS WACKER, Esq. of Law Offices of Ted B. Wacker (Tel: 949-706-7100) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. EMILY GENGE, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani, LLP (Tel: 510-463-8600) for Defendant Checkr, INC.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CHECKR, INC's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Checkr, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled except as to the second cause of action, which plaintiffs have leave to amend if they can do so in good faith.

The FAC pleads that plaintiffs' decedent was killed in a high-speed traffic collision while defendant Andrew Burgher was drunkenly driving for Uber Eats and that Checkr performed a faulty background check on Burgher for Uber. (See, e.g., FAC 15:17-17:2, 23:20-24:5.)

Negligence. Burgher has a criminal history including two driving under the influence convictions not mentioned in Checkr's background report. (See, e.g., FAC 15:1-16:27, 19:1-4.) Checkr argues it was prohibited from "reporting records older than seven years," citing Civil Code 1786.18. (Opp. 4:1-5.) Even assuming the section 1786.18 rule applied, the FAC pleads (and Checkr does not dispute) that Burger was on probation for his second DUI within the seven years - a fact not reported by Checkr. (Id. at 25:25-26:2.) Civil Code 1786.18 does not address probation, which is not parole. Thus, the FAC suffices for the pleading stage. Checkr's other arguments need not be reached as this negligence theory is viable. (Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38-39 [demurrer test is whether count "states a cause of action under any theory"].)

Negligent Hiring, Supervision or Retention. This tort requires an employer-employee relationship (CACI 426), but the FAC pleads none between Burgher and Checkr. (See id. at 28:1-5.)

Wrongful Death/Survival Action. As Checkr acknowledges, these counts are viable if negligence is adequately pled (and it is).(Opp. 6:21-7:4.)

Order signed.
